Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visualising Statistical Analysis

Author

Andy Tai

Published

May 3, 2026

Modified

May 3, 2026

1 Learning Outcome

This hands-on exercise focuses on:

  • ggstatsplot package to create visual graphics with rich statistical information,
  • performance package to visualise model diagnostics, and
  • parameters package to visualise model parameters

2 Getting Started

  • Installing and loading libraries
  • Importing data
  • Understanding the data
  • Converting data types
  • Use the pacman package to check, install and launch the R packages ggstatplot and tidyverse.
  • ggstatsplot is an extension of ggplot2 package for creating graphics with details from statistical tests included in the information-rich plots themselves.
Show the code 
pacman::p_load(ggstatsplot, tidyverse, nortest, ggdist)
  • The code chunk below uses read_csv() of readr package to import Exam_data.csv data file into R and save it as an tibble data frame called exam_data.
Show the code 
exam <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")
  • The data is a tibble dataframe and contains 322 observations across 7 attributes.
Show the code 
glimpse(exam)
Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…
Show the code 
col <- c("CLASS", "GENDER", "RACE")

exam <- exam %>% 
  mutate(across(col, as.factor)) %>% 
  mutate(ID = as.character(ID))

3 Various Statistical Tests

Test What it Measures Function
One-sample Is this sample’s mean/median different from a known value? gghistostats()
Two-sample Are these two groups different from each other? ggbetweenstats()
ANOVA Are any of these groups different from each other? ggbetweenstats()
Correlation Is there a linear relationship between two continuous variables? ggscatterstats()
Association Is there a dependency between two categorical variables? ggbarstats()

3.1 Compare means/medians

  • (a) 1-sample tests
  • (b) 2-sample test
  • (c) 1-way ANOVA
Type Argument Test Used Nature When to Use
type = "parametric" One-sample t-test Parametric When data follows normal distribution — tests against mean
type = "nonparametric" One-sample Wilcoxon signed rank test Non-parametric When data does not follow normal distribution — tests against median
type = "robust" One-sample robust t-test Robust When data has outliers — uses trimmed mean
type = "bayes" Bayes Factor test Bayesian When you want to quantify evidence in favour of null or alternative hypothesis
  • (i) 1-sample t-test
  • (ii) 1-sample Wilcoxon signed rank test
  • (iii) 1-sample robust t-test
  • (iv) Bayes Factor test

\(H_0\): \(\mu = 60\) (English scores mean is equal to 60)

\(H_1\): \(\mu \neq 60\) (English scores mean is not equal to 60)

Show the code 
# Parametric (t-test)
set.seed(1234)
gghistostats(
  data = exam,
  x = ENGLISH,
  type = "parametric",
  test.value = 60,
  xlab = "English scores",
  title = "Parametric: One-sample t-test",
  bin.args = list(fill = "seagreen", 
                  color = "white",
                  alpha = 0.8)
) + theme_bw()

\(H_0\): \(\mu = 60\) (English scores mean is equal to 60)

\(H_1\): \(\mu \neq 60\) (English scores mean is not equal to 60)

Show the code 
# Non-parametric (Wilcoxon)
set.seed(1234)
gghistostats(
  data = exam,
  x = ENGLISH,
  type = "nonparametric",
  test.value = 60,
  xlab = "English scores",
  title = "Non-parametric: Wilcoxon signed rank test",
  bin.args = list(fill = "seagreen", 
                  color = "white",
                  alpha = 0.8)
) + theme_bw()

\(H_0\): \(\mu = 60\) (English scores mean is equal to 60)

\(H_1\): \(\mu \neq 60\) (English scores mean is not equal to 60)

Show the code 
# Robust
set.seed(1234)
gghistostats(
  data = exam,
  x = ENGLISH,
  type = "robust",
  test.value = 60,
  xlab = "English scores",
  title = "Robust: One-sample robust t-test",
  bin.args = list(fill = "seagreen", 
                  color = "white",
                  alpha = 0.8)
) + theme_bw()

  • A Bayes factor is the ratio of the likelihood of one particular hypothesis to the likelihood of another. It can be interpreted as a measure of the strength of evidence in favor of one theory among two competing theories.

  • That’s because the Bayes factor gives us a way to evaluate the data in favor of a null hypothesis, and to use external information to do so. It tells us what the weight of the evidence is in favor of a given hypothesis.

  • When we are comparing two hypotheses: H1 (the alternate hypothesis) and H0 (the null hypothesis), the Bayes Factor is often written as B10, i.e., \[ B_{10} = \frac{P(Data | H_1)}{P(Data | H_0)} \]

  • The Schwarz criterion is one of the easiest ways to calculate rough approximation of the Bayes Factor.

If \(B_{10}\) is… Then you have…
\(> 100\) Extreme evidence for \(H_1\)
\(30 - 100\) Very strong evidence for \(H_1\)
\(10 - 30\) Strong evidence for \(H_1\)
\(3 - 10\) Moderate evidence for \(H_1\)
\(1 - 3\) Anecdotal evidence for \(H_1\)
\(1\) No evidence
\(1/3 - 1\) Anecdotal evidence for \(H_0\)
\(1/3 - 1/10\) Moderate evidence for \(H_0\)
\(1/10 - 1/30\) Strong evidence for \(H_0\)
\(1/30 - 1/100\) Very strong evidence for \(H_0\)
\(< 1/100\) Extreme evidence for \(H_0\)

\(H_0\): \(\mu = 60\)

\(H_1\): \(\mu \neq 60\)

\[B_{10} = \frac{P(Data | H_1)}{P(Data | H_0)}\]

Show the code 
# Bayes
set.seed(1234)
gghistostats(
  data = exam,
  x = ENGLISH,
  type = "bayes",
  test.value = 60,
  xlab = "English scores",
  title = "Bayesian: Bayes Factor test",
  bin.args = list(fill = "seagreen", 
                  color = "white",
                  alpha = 0.8)
) + theme_bw()
Type Argument Test Used Nature
type = "parametric" Welch’s t-test Parametric
type = "nonparametric" or type = "np" Wilcoxon rank-sum test (Mann-Whitney) Non-parametric
type = "robust" Yuen’s trimmed means t-test Robust
type = "bayes" Bayesian t-test Bayesian
  • (i) Welch’s t-test
  • (ii) Wilcoxon rank-sum test
  • (iii) Yuen’s trimmed means t-test
  • (iv) Bayesian t-test

\(H_0\): \(\mu_{Female} = \mu_{Male}\) (Mean Math scores of Female and Male are equal)

\(H_1\): \(\mu_{Female} \neq \mu_{Male}\) (Mean Math scores of Female and Male are not equal)

Show the code 
# Parametric (Welch's t-test)
ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = GENDER,
  y = MATHS,
  type = "parametric",
  title = "Parametric: Welch's t-test",
  messages = FALSE,
  palette = "Set2",
  package = "RColorBrewer"
) + theme_bw()

\(H_0\): \(\mu_{Female} = \mu_{Male}\) (Mean Math scores of Female and Male are equal)

\(H_1\): \(\mu_{Female} \neq \mu_{Male}\) (Mean Math scores of Female and Male are not equal)

Show the code 
# Non-parametric (Wilcoxon rank-sum)
ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = GENDER,
  y = MATHS,
  type = "nonparametric",
  title = "Non-parametric: Wilcoxon rank-sum test",
  messages = FALSE,
  palette = "Set2",
  package = "RColorBrewer"
) + theme_bw()

\(H_0\): \(\mu_{Female} = \mu_{Male}\) (Mean Math scores of Female and Male are equal)

\(H_1\): \(\mu_{Female} \neq \mu_{Male}\) (Mean Math scores of Female and Male are not equal)

Show the code 
# Robust (Yuen's trimmed means)
ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = GENDER,
  y = MATHS,
  type = "robust",
  title = "Robust: Yuen's trimmed means t-test",
  messages = FALSE,
  palette = "Set2",
  package = "RColorBrewer"
) + theme_bw()

\(H_0\): \(\mu_{Female} = \mu_{Male}\)

\(H_1\): \(\mu_{Female} \neq \mu_{Male}\)

\[B_{10} = \frac{P(Data | H_1)}{P(Data | H_0)}\]

Show the code 
# Bayesian
ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = GENDER,
  y = MATHS,
  type = "bayes",
  title = "Bayesian: Bayesian t-test",
  messages = FALSE,
  palette = "Set2",
  package = "RColorBrewer"
) + theme_bw()

\(H_0\): \(\mu_{Chinese} = \mu_{Indian} = \mu_{Malay} = \mu_{Others}\) (Mean English scores of all races are equal)

\(H_1\): \(\mu\) (Not all mean English scores are equal)

Show the code 
ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = RACE, 
  y = ENGLISH,
  type = "p",
  mean.ci=TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE, 
  pairwise.display = "s",  # 'ns': shows only non-sig, 's': shows only sig, 'all': both 
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE
) +
  theme_bw()

3.2 Correlation

  • In the code chunk below, ggscatterstats() is used to build a visual for Significant Test of Correlation between Maths scores and English scores.
  • ggscatterstats() doesn’t have a direct color argument for the points. Use point.args instead to pass colour settings to the underlying geom_point()

\(H_0\): \(\rho = 0\) (There is no correlation between Math and English scores)

\(H_1\): \(\rho \neq 0\) (There is a significant correlation between Math and English scores)

Show the code 
ggscatterstats(
  data = exam,
  x = MATHS,
  y = ENGLISH,
  type = "nonparametric",
  marginal = FALSE,
  point.args = list(color = "rosybrown",
                    size = 2,
                    alpha = 0.6)
) + theme_bw()

3.3 Association

\(H_0\): MATHS_bins and GENDER are independent

\(H_1\): MATHS_bins and GENDER are not independent

  • we need to mutate the data into bins first using cut.
  • From the results, since p-value > 0.05 thus we have not enough statistical evidence to reject the null hypothesis that there is not association between the math_bin and gender variables.
Show the code 
exam1 <- exam %>% 
  mutate(MATHS_bins = 
           cut(MATHS, 
               breaks = c(0,60,75,85,100))
)

ggbarstats(exam1,
            x=MATHS_bins,
            y=GENDER) +
  
  theme_bw()

4 Visualising Models

  • Import libraries and data (ToyotaCorolla.csv)
Show the code 
pacman::p_load(performance, parameters, see)
car_resale <- read_csv("data/ToyotaCorolla.csv")

4.1 Multiple Regression Model using lm()

  • The code chunk below is used to calibrate a multiple linear regression model by using lm() of Base Stats of R.
Show the code 
## transform features
car_resale <- car_resale %>%
  mutate(
    FuelType = as.factor(FuelType),
    MetColor = as.factor(MetColor),
    Automatic = as.factor(Automatic),
    Doors = as.factor(Doors)
  )

## build model
model <- lm(Price ~ Age + 
              KM + 
              FuelType +
              HP +
              MetColor +
              Automatic +
              CC +
              Doors +
              Weight,
            data = car_resale)

4.2 Model Diagnostics Visualisations

  • (a) multicollinearity
  • (b) normality
  • (c) heteroscedasticity
  • (d) complete check
  • In the code chunk, check_collinearity() of performance package.
Show the code 
check_collinearity(model)
# Check for Multicollinearity

Low Correlation

      Term  VIF     VIF 95% CI adj. VIF Tolerance Tolerance 95% CI
       Age 1.94 [ 1.81,  2.10]     1.39      0.51     [0.48, 0.55]
        KM 2.01 [ 1.87,  2.18]     1.42      0.50     [0.46, 0.54]
  MetColor 1.02 [ 1.00,  1.23]     1.01      0.98     [0.81, 1.00]
 Automatic 1.09 [ 1.05,  1.18]     1.05      0.91     [0.85, 0.95]
     Doors 1.32 [ 1.24,  1.41]     1.05      0.76     [0.71, 0.80]
    Weight 3.55 [ 3.25,  3.88]     1.88      0.28     [0.26, 0.31]

Moderate Correlation

 Term  VIF     VIF 95% CI adj. VIF Tolerance Tolerance 95% CI
   HP 6.19 [ 5.64,  6.80]     2.49      0.16     [0.15, 0.18]
   CC 8.66 [ 7.87,  9.54]     2.94      0.12     [0.10, 0.13]

High Correlation

     Term   VIF     VIF 95% CI adj. VIF Tolerance Tolerance 95% CI
 FuelType 16.38 [14.83, 18.09]     2.01      0.06     [0.06, 0.07]
  • render into chart
Show the code 
check_c <- check_collinearity(model)
plot(check_c) + 
  theme_bw()
Show the code 
plot(check_normality(model)) + theme_bw()

In the code chunk, check_heteroscedasticity() of performance package.

Heteroscedasticity refers to a situation where the variance of the errors (or residuals) in the linear regression model is not constant across different levels of the predictor variable(s).

Show the code 
plot(check_heteroscedasticity(model)) + theme_bw()
  • We can also perform the complete check by using check_model().
Show the code 
check_model(model)

4.3 Regression Parameters Visualisations

  • (a) see() method
  • (b) ggcoefstats() method

In the code below, plot() of see package and parameters() of parameters package are used to visualise the parameters of a regression model.

Show the code 
plot(parameters(model)) + theme_bw()

In the code below, ggcoefstats() of ggstatsplot package to visualise the parameters of a regression model.

  • only.significant = TRUE shows labels only for variables with p-value < 0.05, reducing clutter significantly!

  • Alternatively, we can replace only.significant = TRUEwith stats.labels = FALSE. This removes the cluttered statistical annotation boxes and just shows the coefficient dots and confidence interval lines cleanly.

  • Removing non-significant variables can improve the colour contrast of the labels that would otherwise be scaled according to the significance level.

Show the code 
model2 <- lm(Price ~ Age + 
               KM + 
               FuelType +
               HP +
               CC +
               Weight,
             data = car_resale)

ggcoefstats(model2, 
            output = "plot",
            stats.label.args = list(size = 3)) +   
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
        axis.title = element_text(size = 12))

5 References

  • Kam, T.S. (2023). Visual Statistical Analysis.
